file_name: Kaur and Kaur - 2023 - Ti-FCNet Triple fused convolutional neural network-based automated skin lesion classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes Ti-FCNet, a deep learning-based automated skin lesion classification system. It uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for lesion segmentation and a Triple CNN (T-CNN) model fusing features from Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet for classifying melanoma vs. non-melanoma images using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel deep learning model called Ti-FCNet for automated skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology involving CNN-based segmentation, feature extraction using Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet, and classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The authors report original experimental results, including an accuracy of 99.01%, and compare their proposed method against existing models, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Ti‑FCNet: Triple fused convolutional neural network‑based automated skin lesion classification
brief_summary: This study proposes Ti-FCNet, a deep learning framework for automated skin lesion classification to address the challenges of manual diagnosis. The model utilizes the ISIC 2019 dataset, selecting 4,500 melanoma and 4,500 non-melanoma images for segmentation and classification. A CNN segments the lesion regions, followed by data augmentation and pre-processing. Features are extracted using a Triple CNN (T-CNN) architecture fusing Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet, with loss optimized via the Weighted Sum Method. The proposed method achieved an overall accuracy of 99.01%, outperforming existing models like GNSVM and EMREN in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classes in the classification task. 4,500 images selected from the ISIC 2019 dataset. In the confusion matrix, 881 cases were correctly identified as melanoma.
Used as the comparator class in the binary classification task. 4,500 images selected from the ISIC 2019 dataset. In the confusion matrix, 882 cases were correctly identified as non-melanoma.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma classes using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model uses a softmax layer for class prediction and achieves an overall accuracy of 99.01%.
Segmentation of lesion and non-lesion skin regions using a CNN model to extract the region of interest (ROI) prior to classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public challenge dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration comprising dermoscopic skin lesion images. The study selected a subset of 9,000 images (4,500 melanoma and 4,500 non-melanoma) from the total 25,331 images available in the dataset.

use.training: Used for training the CNN segmentation model and the Triple CNN (T-CNN) classification model after data augmentation and pre-processing.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed Ti-FCNet model, with ground truth generated for comparison.
use.other: The original dataset contains 25,331 images; the study utilized a randomly selected subset of 9,000 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

Proposed Model (Ti-FCNet).description: Triple fused CNN using Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet with Weighted Sum Method loss optimization

ISIC 2019.notes: The proposed Ti-FCNet model outperformed all listed baseline and existing models across accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics. The confusion matrix indicates 881 true positives for melanoma and 882 true negatives for non-melanoma out of the tested subset.

architecture_details.backbone: Triple CNN (T-CNN) fusing features from Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet

feature_extraction.Densenet-201: Uses Dense Blocks with dense connections between layers for feature reusing
feature_extraction.ResNet-152: Uses residual blocks with identity association to solve vanishing gradient problems
feature_extraction.SqueezeNet: 18-layer deep CNN with single convolution layer (Con1), eight fire modules (Fire2-9), and nine convolution layers (Con10)

architecture_details.fusion_method: Fusion of features from Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet to generate a single feature vector

training_details.loss_function: Weighted Sum Method (WSM) for T-CNN network loss; Cross-Entropy Loss (CEL) mentioned in formula

preprocessing_techniques: Lesion segmentation using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to separate Region of Interest (ROI) from Non-Region of Interest (NROI)
Manual marking of lesion boundaries to generate ground truth masks
Application of automatic thresholding to separate lesion and non-lesion parts after segmentation
Data augmentation including rotation, scaling, and random cropping
Image rescaling using M-squared normalization to normalize data between -1 and 1 based on minimum and maximum values

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to segmented lesion images to maximize training data size and preserve target labels.
Applied to segmented lesion images to maximize training data size and preserve target labels.
Applied to segmented lesion images to maximize training data size and preserve target labels.
Mentioned as a technique incorporated in the augmentation process to fulfill rotations and translations.
Mentioned as a technique incorporated in the augmentation process, requiring a 3x3 transformation matrix.

conclusions: The proposed Ti-FCNet model, which fuses features from Densenet-201, ResNet-152, and SqueezeNet using a Triple CNN architecture, achieved an overall accuracy of 99.01% in classifying melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions.

Ti-FCNet outperformed existing baseline models (Densenet-201, ResNet-152, SqueezeNet) and other state-of-the-art models (such as EMREN, MDNet, LTDC, EB3-B4, and GNSVM) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, and other performance metrics.

The integration of lesion segmentation using a CNN prior to classification contributed to the model's high performance by effectively isolating the region of interest and reducing the impact of background artifacts.
The use of the Weighted Sum Method (WSM) for loss optimization in the T-CNN network enhanced the overall classification accuracy and minimized error rates.
The proposed method demonstrated superior sensitivity (99.97%) and specificity (98.11%), indicating its potential for effective early detection of skin cancer.

The study acknowledges that existing approaches often suffer from insufficient feature representation, high processing overhead, and increased complexity, which the proposed fusion approach aims to mitigate.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly selected subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (9,000 images: 4,500 melanoma and 4,500 non-melanoma) rather than the full dataset, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability to the broader clinical population.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons, relying solely on descriptive metrics which limits the robustness of the claimed superiority over baseline models.

External validation is absent; the model was evaluated only on the internal test split of the ISIC 2019 dataset, raising concerns about performance on diverse, unseen clinical data or different imaging devices.

The segmentation process relied on 'manual ROI markers' to generate ground truth masks, which introduces potential human bias and inconsistency in the segmentation phase, despite the use of a CNN for segmentation.

The reported accuracy (99.01%) and sensitivity (99.97%) are exceptionally high, which may indicate overfitting to the specific dataset subset or data leakage, especially given the lack of cross-validation details in the provided context.

The comparison with existing models (e.g., GNSVM, EMREN) appears to use metrics reported in other studies rather than re-evaluating them on the exact same train/test split, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid due to differing data distributions.
The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split ratios or the random seed used for selecting the 9,000 images, which hinders reproducibility.

Deployment concerns are not addressed; the study focuses on offline classification performance using MATLAB without discussing computational efficiency, inference time, or integration into clinical workflows.
